Farn Polrrrcnr- PRACrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
November 6,2006
Michael Reiter
Assistant City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
City of Redlands
P. O. Box 3005
Redlands, CA 92373-1505
RE: Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our FiIe No.I-06-191
Dear Mr. Reiter:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Mayor pro Tem patricia
Gilbreath for advice regarding the gift provisions of the politicaiReform Act (the
uAct').r Since your quistiorl. g"i".al in nature and does not refer to a specific factual
situation, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance., pG;;"ote that
nothing in this letter should be construed to evalu ate any conduct that mayhave already
taken place.
You ask whether retirement d;:X;r 
pro rem G,breath,s employer and
presents from clients may be reported as income.
CONCLUSION
Retirement gifts from Mayor Pro Tem Gilbreath's employer and clients must be
reported as gifts and not as income. see discussion below.
FACTS
Mayor Pro Tem Patricia Gilbreath will retire at the end of this calendar year from
Eadie & Payre, LLP, an accounting firm that does business in Redlanar. UrlCilbreath
I Govemment Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83fi4;reguration rgi2g(c)(3),copy enclosed). 
^
.:!:


File No. I-06-191
Page No. 2
expects that she will receive "retirement gifts" from coworkers, clients, and from the
firm. In the past, "retirement gifts" from Eadie &payne,Llp, have included large
screen televisions, golf clubs, and cruise line gift certificates, but not cash.
You wish to know whether the gift limitations in Govemment Code section g9503
applies to retirement gifts given to Ms. Gilbreath byher employer, Eadie &payne,LLp,
and whether such gifts may be reported as income. You also wish to know whetfrer
retirement gifts from Ms. Gilbreath's clients may be reported as income, instead of gifts.
ANALYSIS
The Act imposes certain restrictions and obligations on public officials regarding
the receipt of gifts. As Mayor Pro Tem for the City Jf Realands, pahicia Gilbreath is an
elected officer as that term is defined under section g2ozoof the Act.
Section 89503 provides that no elected state officer, elected officer of a local
government agency, or other individual specified in section 872}0may accept gifts from
any single source_in any calendar yeff worth in excess of the gift limiiif ttre officiat
would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her
statement of economic interests. The current gift Hmlt is $360. (Regulation lgg4o.z.)
In addition, an elected state officer is required to disclose, on an annual statement
of economic interests, the name and address of iach source of gifts aggregating $50 or
more received in a calendar year. (Sections 87200 ,87202-87{Ol *ri\liOn.l * un
elected officer, Mayor pro Tem Gilbreath is subject to these provisions
Section 8202,8 (a) defines **O" as o'any payment that confers a personal benefit
on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received
and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course oibusiness io -"tb.r, of the public without
regard to official status."
There are certain exceptions to tl: definition of "gift" under the Act, including,
among others: informational material, gifts that arenot uJed and returnea wittrin 30 diys
of receipt, and gifts from certain enumerated family members. (Section g202g(b).)
You state in your letter that,'oln the past, 'retirement gifts' from Eadie & payne,
LLP have included large screen televisions, golf clubs and cruise line gift certificates, but
not cash." You also state that Ms. Gilbreath expects that she will receive retirement gifts
from co-workers and clients of the firm.
You ask whgther such gifts may be viewed as consideration for Ms. Gilbreath,s
years of service on behalf of her ernployeE and whether gifts from clients may be viewed
as income.


Your facts do not indicatethatthere.was any formal companypolicyregarding
such gifts as consideration for employment.3 You also do not provide *y otho details
with regard to potential gifts from Ms. Gilbreath's clients. As a result, thte is no basis
for a conclusion that such gifts can be considered partof an employ.";. compensation
package.
Payments, in the form of retirement presents-either frgm a corporation, such as
Ms. Gilbreath's employer, or her clients-absent an exceptio n,o are.o".iJ"rlJ srt.
under the Act. Therefore, based on the facts you have provided, such prererris do ,ot
appear to fall under any exemption under the Act, and thus would be subject to gift
reporting rules and gift limits.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
File No. I-06-t9t
Page No. 3
Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Divi sion

salary in that an employee agrees to receive a,smallerpayment in eichange forreceiving the remaining
portion ofhis other compensation by way of these fringe tenefits. ThesJhave included-fringe benefits
such as pensionplans, health benefits, deferred compeisation, tax sheltered annuities, tife iniurance ,leave,
etc'- Qn re Moore (1976) 3 FPPC ops' 33,36.) You have not'proviJed any information that would indicate
such retirement gifts would be considered "fringe benefits" ortu.. Gilbreath,s "*d;d;,. 
Therefore,
such paynents would be considered gifts, and not income.
a Section 89503(e) provides that gifts exchanged between individuals (other than lobblsts) on
holidays, birthdays, or other similar o".uriorrs are not prohibited or limited as long as the gifts exchanged
are not substantially disproportionate in value. Regulation rc9a2@)(8) provides tiuigift.'";Jlrurg"a
between an official and any_ other individual (except a lobbfist) o" t ofa-ryr, birttrday{ oi.i[r-
occasions, which are not substantially disproportio-nate in vatue, are not subject to limits and need not be
reported as gifts on the official's statement oieconomic interests. Recent amendments to this regtrlation
were intended to be broad enough to cover a range of occasions such as weddings, retiremeniparties, the
birth, adoption, or baptism of a child, or even memorial services, where gifts are customarily exchanged.
Thus, if Ms. Gilbreath "exchanges" gifts (as provided under regulation tse+z1a;1s; *tr, iotiriaoals who
provide her with retirement_presents, such gifts would not be sribject to reporting'rolm o, the annual gift
limit of $360 per source. Please note, howiver, that this .*".ptiJ., Jors not apply to gifts provided by
lobbyists or non-individuals, such as corporations and organriations.
